channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Fallout of War Piles Economic Pain Onto Europe’s Political Stress
item.description: Europe is finding itself on the outs with Russia, China and the U.S., in what’s amounting to its very own “Mean Girls” moment.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Patrick Junker for The New York Times
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas terminal in Germany. Most of that L.N.G. comes from the United States, underscoring Europe’s vulnerability to American supplies.

item.title: Banks Are Warned About Anthropic’s New, Powerful A.I. Technology
item.description: In an unusual move, the Treasury secretary and the Federal Reserve chair gathered bank executives to caution about cyberthreats posed by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The stark message was delivered by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent in a hastily called meeting.

item.title: Fred Drasner, a Feisty Leader of The Daily News, Is Dead at 83
item.description: A former New York cabdriver who never lost his edge, he was co-publisher alongside Mort Zuckerman as he took on rival papers in the so-called tabloid wars.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Fred Drasner in about 2000. He and Mort Zuckerman bought the flailing Daily News in 1993, then brought it back to profit through painful cost cutting, newsroom investments and an aggressive marketing campaign.

item.title: Pentagon Appeals Court Decisions Gutting Its Press Restrictions
item.description: The Defense Department filed a formal notice that it intended to fight a federal judge’s recent rulings that its press restrictions were unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: In March, Judge Paul Friedman sided with The New York Times and tossed out key provisions of the Pentagon’s press policy.

item.title: European Airports Warn of Jet Fuel Shortages if Strait of Hormuz Remains Shut
item.description: An association of airports told European Union officials that fuel shipments through the Strait of Hormuz had to restart within three weeks to avoid a “systemic” shortage.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Travelers at Frankfurt Airport in Germany on Friday.

item.title: Bob Law, Trailblazing Talk-Radio Host, Dies at 86
item.description: His wee-hours show “Night Talk” was a nationally syndicated stalwart, examining tough issues and spotlighting Black cultural and political stars.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
item.media:description: Bob Law in 2006. “The great value of Black talk radio is its ability to give voice to a community that would otherwise be voiceless,” he wrote.

item.title: Iran War Drives Deeper Oil Shock Than Prices Reveal
item.description: The war with Iran is preventing huge amounts of oil from flowing out of the Persian Gulf, but the prices that many people track don’t fully capture the scale of the disruption.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas

item.title: Bessent and Powell’s A.I. Anxiety
item.description: The Treasury secretary and the Fed chairman reportedly summoned banking leaders to discuss the potential systemic risks of Anthropic’s new model.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount, Lauren Hirsch and Ken Belson
item.media:credit: Jeff McIntosh/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, left, and Jay Powell, the Fed chair, worry that new A.I. tools could pose risks to the U.S. financial system.

item.title: A Record Jump in U.S. Gasoline Prices Is Squeezing Consumers
item.description: The cost at the pump made its biggest monthly percentage increase in decades amid lingering tensions over the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: The average cost of a gallon of regular gasoline was $3.64 in March, up from $2.91 in February.

item.title: The Iran War Has Prompted Some Companies to Raise Prices
item.description: Delta Air Lines, Amazon, the United States Postal Service and others have said they are raising prices amid higher energy costs connected to the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines said it had raised baggage fees by $10 for the first and second bags, and by $50 for a third bag, for domestic and select international routes.

item.title: Europe Braces for a Spike in Inflation
item.description: Investors are betting that the European Central Bank and the Bank of England will raise interest rates this year.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Christine Lagarde, the president of the European Central Bank, has warned that the economic effects of the war with Iran will linger.

item.title: U.S. Inflation Surged in March as Iran War Pushed Up Prices
item.description: Soaring energy costs led to the biggest monthly increase in the Consumer Price Index since the peak of the post-pandemic inflation crisis in June 2022.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump Administration Returns to Court for Yet Another Tariff Lawsuit
item.description: States and small businesses challenged the 10 percent tax on many imports that President Trump imposed after the Supreme Court struck down a previous slate of tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: The legal wrangling over President Trump’s new tariffs affirmed the stakes of his continued trade brinkmanship at a fragile moment for the global economy.

item.title: TSA Lines Are Shorter. The World Cup and a Lengthy Shutdown Could Change That.
item.description: Security lines are shorter, but the shutdown continues and pay is unresolved. With the World Cup around the corner, T.S.A. agents are tempering their expectations.
item.dc:creator: Ceylan Yeğinsu
item.media:description: Back pay for Transportation Security Administration agents has recently been restored, but questions remain about future paychecks as the partial government shutdown continues. Above, T.S.A. employees at Ronald Reagan National Airport in the Washington area on March 30.

item.title: Consumer Spending, Engine of the U.S. Economy, Is Under Strain
item.description: Higher fuel costs are raising food and travel prices, while a shaky stock market tamps down free spenders.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: “You go into the grocery store, you buy the things you normally would, and then all of a sudden it’s $20 or $30 more there,” said Angie Howard, who lives in Portland, Ore.

item.title: Trump’s Changes Lock Some Employers Out of H-1B Visa Program
item.description: Since imposing a $100,000 fee on new visas in September, the Trump administration has upended the skilled worker program.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sara McCabe, the president of the Wayside Youth & Family Support Network, a nonprofit that has struggled in recent years to recruit teachers for its special education school.

item.title: How Airlines Turned First-Class Seats From Freebies to a Profit Engine
item.description: Airlines used to give away most of their nicest seats, but they have increasingly found ways to persuade people to pay a lot for them.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi

item.title: Middle East War Triggers Higher Prices in China
item.description: Three and a half years of deflationary pressure on Chinese factories reversed course last month as higher energy prices cycled into the economy.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: A car factory in Chongqing, China. Falling wholesale prices have forced thousands of manufacturers to sell their goods for less and less.

item.title: Stocks Waver Ahead of Cease-Fire Talks, but Log Big Weekly Gains
item.description: The S&P 500 rose 3.6 percent, its biggest weekly gain since last year. Investors are bracing for developments in peace talks between the United States and Iran, set for the weekend.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Judge Rejects Hegseth’s Second Attempt to Restrict Reporters at Pentagon
item.description: A federal judge gutted a set of rules that were adopted after the court declared an earlier press policy unconstitutional, in a case brought by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A federal judge in Washington said the Pentagon’s revised press policy was essentially unconstitutional.

item.title: Labor Secretary Faces Civil Rights Complaints From Department Staff
item.description: Three employees described a hostile work environment under Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civil rights complaints against Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer were filed in recent weeks by three women who worked in the secretary’s executive office.

item.title: White House and Crypto Industry Fight Bank Lobby Over Stablecoin Income
item.description: The Trump administration backed a proposal for stablecoin issuers to offer yield to investors.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: White House Council of Economic Advisers
item.media:description: President Trump signed an act into law last summer that gave the cryptocurrency industry the green light to more closely merge with traditional finance.

item.title: Volkswagen to End E.V. Production at Tennessee Plant
item.description: The German carmaker is the latest to scale back plans for electric vehicles in favor of gasoline models.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volkswagen said it would stop U.S. production of the ID.4, an electric sport utility vehicle.

item.title: I.M.F. Says Iran War Will Drag Global Growth Lower
item.description: Kristalina Georgieva, the head of the International Monetary Fund, said the war in the Middle East could lead to another bout of inflation and higher interest rates.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Buildings of Shahid Beheshti University in Tehran damaged by airstrikes last week. The International Monetary Fund said on Thursday that the war would cause slower growth in the world economy this year.

item.title: David B. Cornstein, Envoy Who Built U.S. Ties to Orban, Dies at 87
item.description: A former jewelry-counter magnate, he served in Hungary under his friend President Trump, strengthening relations between the two countries as Orban tilted rightward.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: David Cornstein in 2001, when he was active in Republican politics in New York. Later, as ambassador to Hungary under President Trump, he joined in abandoning the Obama administration’s hard-line approach to Mr. Orban’s illiberal governance and tried flattery instead.

item.title: America’s Furniture Stores Struggle to Survive a Frozen Housing Market
item.description: Retailers are going bankrupt and liquidating as record-low housing turnover leaves fewer customers looking to furnish homes.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin

item.title: How the Strait of Hormuz Stifled a Market Rally
item.description: Questions about how freely ships can pass through the waterway have pushed up oil prices and weighed on stocks.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Transit through the Strait of Hormuz remains perhaps the defining question of the tenuous U.S.-Iran cease-fire.

item.title: Fed’s Inflation Woes Preceded the War With Iran
item.description: An energy shock stemming from the fighting has added a layer of complexity to the Federal Reserve’s decision-making around interest rates.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: An oil facility in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, after an attack last month. Energy-related price increases are expected to worsen U.S. inflation.

item.title: Markets Have Faced a Year of Chaos and Still Done Awfully Well
item.description: Most stock investors have lost money during the Iran war, but returns have been splendid for the year since the “Liberation Day” tariff announcement.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: A Must for the Next Food Craze? Be ‘Social Media Gorgeous.’
item.description: The ascent of ube has little to do with the purple yam’s taste or Filipino origins. It’s the color, flavor experts say.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times

item.title: Oil Prices Edge Higher as Confidence in Cease-Fire Wavers
item.description: Investors were in a more sober mood after sharp moves on the previous day.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Half of Gen Z Uses AI, but Their Feelings Are Souring, Study Shows
item.description: A new study from Gallup found that young adults have grown less hopeful and more angry about artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Andria Lo for The New York Times

item.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz Reopening? What to Know Under Iran-Cease Fire.
item.description: Vessels are wary of passing the coast of Iran in the strait, given the fragile agreement, and the number of ships traveling through it has even dropped.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels off Oman on Wednesday. Iran has said ships that want to pass must coordinate with its armed forces.

item.title: White House Secures Foreign Steel for Trump’s Ballroom Project
item.description: ArcelorMittal, a European steel maker, is donating tens of millions of dollars of foreign steel for President Trump’s new ballroom.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Luke Broadwater
item.media:description: The construction site for the new White House ballroom last month.

item.title: Fed Minutes Show Officials in No Rush to Cut as Iran War Scrambled Outlook
item.description: The conflict in the Middle East has left the Federal Reserve braced for higher inflation, with more officials open to the possibility of rate increases.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall, but Energy Firms Remain Frozen After U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: Analysts said oil and natural gas energy companies would not quickly restore production unless attacks stopped and ships started moving through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Tankers anchored last month off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz. Until shipping traffic starts moving again, oil and gas companies are not going to reopen the taps.

item.title: Adam Back Denies He Is Satoshi Nakamoto in Response to Times Investigation
item.description: “Dr. Adam Back has consistently stated that he is not Satoshi Nakamoto,” his company said in a statement. “What is not speculative is Adam’s foundational contribution to Bitcoin.”
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adam Back onstage at a conference in Miami in February.

item.title: Amazon and U.S. Postal Service Reach New Deal on Deliveries After Year of Talks
item.description: Amazon will cut the volume of packages it ships through the Postal Service by 20 percent under a tentative new deal that will preserve a crucial source of income for the agency.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross and Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: Justin T. Gellerson for The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S.P.S. relies on Amazon for billions of dollars in revenue and has long struggled to stabilize its finances.

item.title: Why Gas Prices Won’t Fall as Quickly as Oil Prices
item.description: The energy industry refers to the behavior of gasoline prices as “up like a rocket, down like a feather.”
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: It could take time for U.S. gasoline prices to come down.

item.title: Jeff Shell Steps Down as President of Paramount
item.description: Mr. Shell, the former chief executive of NBCUniversal, is leaving after becoming entangled in a legal battle with a professional gambler, R.J. Cipriani.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Stella Kalinina for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paramount is trying to finalize a merger of roughly $111 billion with Warner Bros. Discovery, a deal that could reshape Hollywood.

item.title: Blue Collar Work Has Plateaued, Narrowing Options for Young Workers
item.description: Skilled electricians, plumbers and factory workers are in demand, but job openings have dropped.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Business for Electric Avenue has picked up in recent months.

item.title: Greece Plans to Block Social Media for Children Under 15
item.description: Countries including Australia, Spain and others have already passed bans or are working on plans to restrict teenagers’ access to social sites.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis
item.media:description: Children checking their tablets and smartphones at a summer camp in Agios Andreas, Greece, last year.

item.title: ProPublica Journalists Strike for a Day, Partly Over A.I.
item.description: Other sticking points for journalists at the investigative nonprofit included demands for wage increases and layoff protections.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Picketing outside ProPublica’s Manhattan office. About 150 ProPublica workers walked off the job on Wednesday.

item.title: After Iran Cease-Fire, Ship Traffic in Strait of Hormuz Remains Throttled
item.description: A handful of vessels have crossed the crucial waterway since the U.S.-Iran truce began, but shipowners, insurers and others are wary of safe passage.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Cargo ships near the Strait of Hormuz last month. Traffic through the strait has slowed to a trickle during the war.

item.title: Investors Still Have Questions About the Cease-Fire
item.description: Analysts and traders are anxiously watching the Strait of Hormuz and whether peace talks can resolve big differences between the United States and Iran.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Stock markets worldwide jumped today after the U.S. and Iran reached a cease-fire.

item.title: Delta Expects Strong Profit Despite Higher Fuel Costs
item.description: The airline said demand for tickets remained very strong despite the effects of the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines said it expected to earn a profit of about $1 billion, before taxes, in the quarter ending in June.

item.title: Gas Prices Won’t Quickly Return to Prewar Levels, Even if the Strait of Hormuz Reopens
item.description: Some wells can be turned on in days or weeks, but bringing the Gulf’s energy system back to something akin to normal will take months.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Unraveling the Mystery Behind Bitcoin’s Creator
item.description: Our investigative reporter John Carreyrou spent 18 months digging through the archives of online cryptography communities in search of the identity of Satoshi Nakamoto, the anonymous inventor of bitcoin.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou, Sutton Raphael, James Surdam, Coleman Lowndes and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: Oil Prices Plunge and Stocks Surge After Cease-Fire Deal
item.description: Despite confusions about the terms of the deal, investors on Wednesday welcomed the move toward de-escalation between Iran and the United States.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
